metadata.title: mm to px (DPI) converter – convert millimeters to pixels
metadata.description: Convert millimeters to pixels at any DPI. Essential for print design, packaging, and preparing files in Illustrator, InDesign, and CorelDRAW.

hero.title: Convert millimeters to pixels at any DPI
hero.description: Enter a value in millimeters, set the DPI resolution, and read the result in pixels. Check how many pixels a given value in millimeters corresponds to, e.g. the dimensions of a business card, flyer, poster, or A4 sheet. The converter helps you pick the right file resolution before sending it to a print shop.

software.name: Millimeter to pixel converter with adjustable DPI – mm to px
software.description: Convert millimeters to pixels with an adjustable DPI value. As soon as you enter a dimension, you instantly see how many pixels it corresponds to, including standard business card, flyer, and A-series paper sizes. The converter is built for preparing print-ready files and packaging design, and runs entirely in the browser with no sign-up.
featureList: Conversion works both ways, so you can convert millimeters to pixels and pixels to millimeters
The result appears instantly as you type, no button to click
Copy the result to your clipboard in one click
An extensive reference table for values from 1 to 1,000 millimeters at five DPI resolutions
A separate format table covers business cards, DL flyers, A0–A7 paper, and large-format posters
A how-to section explains setting mm to px in Illustrator, InDesign, CorelDRAW, Photoshop, Figma, and Canva
A short technical note on the mm unit in CSS, SVG, and web code
All calculations run locally in your browser; your data never leaves your device and is never stored
The exact conversion formula is px = mm × DPI ÷ 25.4, based on the fixed length of an inch (25.4 mm)

howTo.name: How to convert millimeters to pixels
howTo.description: Quick instructions for using the mm to px converter with adjustable DPI.

steps.name: Enter a value in millimeters
steps.text: Type the number of millimeters you want to convert to pixels in the left field.

steps.name: Set the DPI resolution
steps.text: Choose a DPI value. Screens typically use 96 DPI, while print uses 300 DPI.

steps.name: Read the result or reverse the direction
steps.text: The result in pixels appears instantly in the field next to it. You can copy it or use the Reverse button to convert pixels to millimeters instead.

contentBlocks.title: Why convert millimeters to pixels?
contentBlocks.html:
The millimeter is the most precise physical unit used in print, packaging design, and engineering — print shops specify business card, flyer, and bleed dimensions in millimeters. The pixel, on the other hand, describes a single point on a screen or in an image file. To convert one to the other, you need to know the DPI resolution: the number of pixels packed into one inch of material.

This conversion is used most often by designers preparing files for a print shop, packaging designers checking label dimensions, and anyone working in Illustrator, InDesign, or CorelDRAW, who works in millimeters but has to deliver a file with an exact pixel count. The converter saves you from doing the math by hand and remembering the formula.

Further down this page you will find an extensive reference table for popular millimeter values, a reverse table from pixels to millimeters, ready-made dimensions for business cards, flyers, and A0–A7 paper sizes, and short guides on switching millimeters to pixels in Illustrator, InDesign, CorelDRAW, Photoshop, Figma, and Canva.
contentBlocks.imageAlt: Free millimeter to pixel converter – Arteon

contentBlocks.title: How does mm to px conversion work? Formula and example
contentBlocks.html:
The whole conversion comes down to one formula, based on the fixed relationship between an inch and a millimeter: one inch is exactly 25.4 mm. So you just multiply the number of millimeters by your chosen DPI resolution and divide by 25.4.

The full formula is: pixels = millimeters × DPI ÷ 25.4. The converter performs this calculation instantly, so you never have to do the math or hunt for a calculator.

Example: say you want to know how many pixels a 90 mm wide business card has at a print resolution of 300 DPI. Plugging the values into the formula gives 90 × 300 ÷ 25.4, which is exactly 1,063 pixels. The same card displayed on a 96 DPI screen would only take up 340 pixels, because fewer points fit per inch.

The reverse direction works the same way: millimeters = pixels × 25.4 ÷ DPI. That lets you check exactly how large a file with a width of, say, 1,181 pixels would be physically, or whether an image from the web is large enough for a specific print size.

contentBlocks.title: How many pixels do different millimeter values produce at different DPI?
contentBlocks.description: The table below shows how the same dimensions in millimeters translate to pixels at the five most commonly used resolutions. A few values are worth remembering, since they match real formats exactly: 35 mm at 300 DPI is the short edge of a passport photo, 90 mm is the width of a standard business card, and 210 mm is the width of an A4 sheet.
contentBlocks.caption: All values are rounded to the nearest whole number, following the formula px = mm × DPI ÷ 25.4.

contentBlocks.title: How many millimeters do different pixel values produce at different DPI?
contentBlocks.description: Sometimes you start from a pixel count, for example a known screen resolution, and need to check how many millimeters that is. The table below shows that conversion for the four most commonly used resolutions, including the full Full HD screen.
contentBlocks.caption: The pair 1,920 and 1,080 pixels is a full Full HD screen — at a 1:1 scale and 96 DPI it physically measures 508 × 285.75 mm, or roughly half a meter wide. If you convert this direction more often, we have a dedicated px to mm calculator with an even wider range of values.

contentBlocks.title: The most popular print formats and their pixel dimensions
contentBlocks.description: If instead of a single millimeter value you need the dimensions of a finished format (for example a business card, a DL flyer, or an A4 sheet), the table below lists the values at the two most commonly used resolutions. Dimensions are always given as width × height.
contentBlocks.caption: A payment card in the ISO ID-1 standard has an official size of 85.60 × 53.98 mm — a more precise figure than the common 85 × 55 mm rounding used for European business cards.

contentBlocks.title: How do you convert millimeters to pixels in popular apps?

items.title: Illustrator
items.description: Adobe Illustrator works in whatever unit you set when creating the document. Change it under Edit › Preferences › Units, picking Millimeters for print materials. The pixel result depends on the artboard resolution set at the start of the project — if the file is headed to a print shop, set it to 300 DPI. If you are working on a flyer in mm but need pixel dimensions for an on-screen preview, the easiest way is to copy the dimension from our converter and type it into the Transform panel.

items.title: InDesign
items.description: Adobe InDesign is the standard tool for laying out multi-page publications and works in millimeters by default. Change the unit under Edit › Preferences › Units & Increments. When exporting a PDF for print, the program automatically converts the page dimensions to pixels based on the set resolution — 300 DPI by default for offset printing. Remember to add bleed (usually 3 mm), which you set directly in the export dialog.

items.title: CorelDRAW
items.description: CorelDRAW is the standard tool in many print shops and ad agencies. Change the document unit on the property bar at the top of the screen by picking millimeters from the dropdown next to the ruler icon. Set the export resolution in the Export dialog (Ctrl+E) when saving to JPG, PNG, or TIFF — that same dialog shows how many pixels the resulting file will have at the chosen DPI.

items.title: Photoshop
items.description: In Adobe Photoshop, set the canvas dimensions in the Image › Image Size dialog. Choose Millimeters from the unit dropdown, enter the physical dimension, and set the target DPI in the Resolution field — Photoshop converts the value to pixels automatically. Uncheck Resample if you only want to change how the dimensions are displayed without affecting the actual pixel count.

items.title: Figma
items.description: Figma works exclusively in pixels at a fixed 96 DPI, so there is no option to switch the unit to millimeters. To design something destined for print, first convert the physical dimension to pixels in our converter at your target DPI (usually 300), and only then enter that value when creating a new frame.

items.title: Canva
items.description: In Canva, click Create a design › Custom size and pick the mm unit to enter a physical dimension — Canva converts it to pixels automatically on export. If the file is going to a print shop, enable the print PDF format plus bleed and crop marks in the download options. Canva exports print files at 300 DPI by default.

items.title: CSS, SVG, and web code
items.description: In CSS, the mm unit is converted with the fixed formula 1mm = 96px ÷ 25.4 ≈ 3.78px and does not change based on the device. The same applies to SVG files, where dimensions can be set directly in millimeters in the width/height attribute — the browser converts them to pixels with the same constant. In practice, web interfaces use px, rem, or em, and mm is reserved for print stylesheets (@media print) or vector files headed for further editing in design apps.

items.title: Preparing files for a print shop
items.description: You can check that a DL flyer (99 × 210 mm) for digital print at 300 DPI is exactly 1,169 × 2,480 px, and that a 90 × 50 mm business card is 1,063 × 591 px. The converter helps you avoid a print shop rejecting your file for insufficient resolution.

items.title: Calculating bleed
items.description: Most print shops require a 3 mm bleed on every edge. At 300 DPI, that is an extra 35 px per side — enter the sum of the dimension and twice the bleed into the converter to immediately get the target pixel count.

items.title: Packaging and label design
items.description: A jar label 120 mm wide at 300 DPI is 1,417 px. The converter helps you quickly check label, sticker, and packaging dimensions before sending files to an offset or digital print shop.

items.title: ID photos
items.description: An EU passport or ID photo is 35 × 45 mm, which is exactly 413 × 531 px at 300 DPI. You can use the same approach to check pixel dimensions for biometric photos required for other official documents.

contentBlocks.title: Comparison of millimeter and pixel units
Frequently asked questions about mm to px conversion

items.question: Is 1 pixel the same as 1 millimeter?
items.answer: No, that is a common misconception. A pixel and a millimeter are two different units — one digital, one physical — and the relationship between them changes with the DPI resolution. At the standard 96 DPI screen, one millimeter is about 3.78 pixels, while at 300 DPI print resolution it is nearly 11.81 pixels. There is no single fixed value of "1 mm = 1 px" that applies universally.

items.question: How many pixels is 1 millimeter?
items.answer: It depends entirely on the resolution you set. At the standard 96 DPI screen resolution, one millimeter is about 3.78 pixels, and at the 300 DPI used for print, 11.81 pixels. To convert any number of millimeters to pixels, multiply it by the DPI and divide by 25.4.

items.question: What is the formula for converting mm to px?
items.answer: The formula comes from a fixed relationship: one inch is exactly 25.4 mm. The full formula is pixels = millimeters × DPI ÷ 25.4. It also works in reverse: millimeters = pixels × 25.4 ÷ DPI. You only need two values — the dimension and the target resolution — to instantly calculate the third.

items.question: How do I convert millimeters to pixels?
items.answer: The simplest way is to type the dimension in millimeters into the converter field and pick the DPI you need — the result appears instantly. If you would rather calculate it by hand, use the formula: pixels = millimeters × DPI ÷ 25.4. For 20 mm at 96 DPI, you get 20 × 96 ÷ 25.4, or about 76 pixels.

items.question: How many pixels is 20 mm?
items.answer: Twenty millimeters on a 96 DPI screen is about 76 pixels. At 300 DPI print resolution, the same value grows to 236 pixels, and at 600 DPI used for high-quality photo printing it reaches 472 pixels.

items.question: How many pixels is 100 millimeters?
items.answer: One hundred millimeters (10 cm) at 96 DPI is about 378 pixels. At 300 DPI print resolution, the same 100 mm is exactly 1,181 pixels — a value worth remembering, since it is the short edge of a classic 10×15 cm photo print.

items.question: What is px in millimeters and what does it measure?
items.answer: A pixel (px) is a digital unit, not a physical length measurement — it has no fixed millimeter equivalent until you set a DPI or PPI resolution. In practice, 1 pixel at 96 DPI (screen standard) is about 0.265 mm, while at 300 DPI (print standard) it is only 0.085 mm.

items.question: How many millimeters is 40 pixels?
items.answer: Forty pixels on a 96 DPI screen is about 10.58 mm, just over 1 cm. At 300 DPI print resolution, the same number of pixels only takes up 3.39 mm. The higher the DPI, the less physical space a given number of pixels occupies.

items.question: Can I convert pixels back to millimeters?
items.answer: Yes, our converter works in both directions. Type the number of pixels into the right field, or use the Reverse button to flip the conversion direction — the result in millimeters appears instantly. The formula for this direction is millimeters = pixels × 25.4 ÷ DPI.

items.question: What pixel dimensions does a business card have?
items.answer: A Polish business card at 90 × 50 mm is 1,063 × 591 px at 300 DPI. A European business card at 85 × 55 mm is 1,004 × 650 px at the same resolution. After adding the standard 3 mm bleed on every edge, the dimensions grow to 96 × 56 mm, or about 1,134 × 661 px.

items.question: What is bleed and how many pixels do I add?
items.answer: Bleed is extra margin, usually 3–5 mm, around your design that gets trimmed off after printing. It prevents white edges if the cutting machine is slightly off. At 300 DPI, the standard 3 mm bleed is an extra 35 pixels per side — that is how much bigger your file should be than the final trimmed format.

items.question: How many pixels does an A4 sheet have in millimeters?
items.answer: An A4 sheet is 210 × 297 mm, which is exactly 2,480 × 3,508 pixels at 300 DPI. At 150 DPI, the same dimensions are 1,240 × 1,754 pixels, and at a screen resolution of 96 DPI just 794 × 1,123 pixels.

items.question: What DPI should I use for printing flyers and business cards?
items.answer: 300 DPI is the standard for both offset and digital printing — most print shops require this value. For large formats like A1 or A0 posters, 150 DPI is often enough, since they are viewed from a greater distance. Large-format banners and billboards are sometimes printed at just 30–72 DPI.

items.question: How do I set mm to px in Illustrator or InDesign?
items.answer: In Adobe Illustrator, go to Edit › Preferences › Units and choose Millimeters — the unit changes across all panels and rulers immediately. InDesign works similarly and uses millimeters by default. In both programs, the resulting pixel count depends on the resolution set when exporting the file, usually 300 DPI for print. You will find detailed guides for Illustrator, InDesign, CorelDRAW, Photoshop, Figma, and Canva in the section above.

items.question: What is the difference between DPI and PPI?
items.answer: DPI (dots per inch) is the number of ink dots per inch of material and is a printer specification. PPI (pixels per inch) is the number of pixels per inch of screen and describes a monitor or display. In everyday use, designers use both terms interchangeably, but technically DPI applies to print and PPI to screens.

items.question: How do I convert millimeters to pixels in CSS or SVG?
items.answer: In CSS stylesheets the mm unit is converted with a fixed formula: 1mm = 96px ÷ 25.4 ≈ 3.78px, regardless of the device. The same constant applies in SVG files, where dimensions can be written directly in millimeters. In practice, web interfaces use px, rem or em units, while mm is reserved for print stylesheets (@media print).

items.question: What is the exact size of a payment card in pixels?
items.answer: The ISO ID-1 standard, which applies to payment cards, SIM cards, and ID cards, is exactly 85.60 × 53.98 mm. At 300 DPI, that comes out to 1,011 × 638 pixels. This is a more precise figure than the common 85 × 55 mm rounding sometimes used for European business cards.
